title: Sequences of Real Numbers
description: Module 1 — From the ε–N definition to Cauchy completeness

nodes.label: Limit Point of a Set\n\((x-\varepsilon, x+\varepsilon) \cap A \setminus\{x\} \ne \emptyset\)
Order Limit Theorem\n\(a_n \le b_n \Rightarrow \lim a_n \le \lim b_n\)

edges.label: in \(a_n = ar^{n-1}\), the ratio between consecutive terms is \_\_\_
edges.hint: Compute \(a_{n+1}/a_n\). The constant you get plays the role of common ___.

edges.label: "\(\forall \varepsilon > 0\,\, \exists N\,\, \forall n>N\,\, |a_n - L| < \varepsilon\)" defines the \_\_\_
edges.hint: This formal statement is the ε–N definition of the ___.

edges.label: no real \(L\) makes \(|a_n - L|\) eventually small — the seq fails to \_\_\_
edges.hint: The OPPOSITE of what we want — the seq fails to ___.

edges.label: a point every \(\varepsilon\)-nbhd of which contains \_\_\_ many points of \(A\)
edges.hint: Equivalent characterization: every neighborhood contains ___ many points of \(A\).

edges.label: a convergent sequence has \_\_\_ limit
edges.hint: How many limits can a convergent sequence have?

edges.label: \(\lim(a_n + b_n) = \lim a_n + \lim b_n\) is the \_\_\_ rule
edges.hint: Which limit law is this?

edges.label: if \(b_n \le a_n \le c_n\) and \(b_n, c_n \to L\), then \(a_n \to\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: The two outer sequences pin down the middle one.

edges.label: if \(a_n \le b_n\) for all \(n\), then \(\lim a_n\) \_\_\_ \(\lim b_n\)
edges.hint: Strict inequality is NOT preserved in the limit; only the weak one is.

edges.label: dropping the first \(k\) terms \_\_\_ the limit
edges.hint: Finitely many terms cannot affect the tail behavior.

edges.label: past some \(N\), every \(a_n\) lies in \((L-1, L+1)\) — so \(\{a_n\}\) is \_\_\_
edges.hint: There is a single \(M\) with \(|a_n| \le M\) for all \(n\) — call this property ___.

edges.label: so it satisfies \(|a_n| \le\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: A constant upper bound — call it \(M\).

edges.label: always-increasing OR always-decreasing — one Greek word for "one direction": \_\_\_
edges.hint: mono- = "one", -tone = "direction/tension".

edges.label: every nonempty bounded-above subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) has a \_\_\_
edges.hint: AKA "least upper bound".

edges.label: MCT conclusion: bounded + monotone \(\Rightarrow\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: What MCT GIVES us, given the two hypotheses.

edges.label: MCT hypothesis: monotone \(+\) \_\_\_ \(\Rightarrow\) convergent
edges.hint: The OTHER hypothesis MCT needs.

edges.label: AM–GM forces \(x_{n+1} \ge\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Apply AM–GM to \(x_n\) and \(c/x_n\): their geometric mean is \(\sqrt{c}\).

edges.label: iteration converges because it is decreasing and bounded \_\_\_
edges.hint: It's bounded below by \(\sqrt{c}\) — so MCT applies.

edges.label: iterating arith. and geom. means of \(a_1,b_1\) gives sequences with the \_\_\_ limit
edges.hint: \(b_{n+1} - a_{n+1} \to 0\), so they share their limit.

edges.label: pick STRICTLY \_\_\_ indices \(n_1 < n_2 < \cdots\) to extract a subsequence
edges.hint: The indices must go in one direction only — strictly ___.

edges.label: a sequence may have \_\_\_ subsequential limits, e.g. \((-1)^n\) has two
edges.hint: \((-1)^n\) has subsequential limits \(+1\) and \(-1\) — so a sequence can have ___ of them.

edges.label: \(\{a_n\}\) converges \(\iff\) \(\{a_{2k}\}\) and \(\{a_{2k+1}\}\) share the \_\_\_ limit
edges.hint: Both even-indexed and odd-indexed subsequences must converge to the ___ value.

edges.label: if \(a_n \to L\), then \(\sigma_n \to\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Averaging preserves the limit (HW 2004, Cesàro).

edges.label: index \(n\) such that NO later term exceeds \(a_n\) — visualize a mountain \_\_\_
edges.hint: Highest point with nothing taller to its right — a mountain ___.

edges.label: inf-many peaks ⟹ ↘ subseq; finitely many ⟹ ↗ subseq — so EVERY seq has one going \_\_\_
edges.hint: Either way, you get a subsequence that consistently goes ___.

edges.label: monotone subseq + boundedness + \_\_\_ \(\Rightarrow\) BW
edges.hint: Apply this theorem to the monotone subsequence to extract a convergent one.

edges.label: BW hypothesis: every \_\_\_ sequence has a convergent subsequence
edges.hint: The single hypothesis BW needs.

edges.label: if \(a_n \to L\), then \(|a_m - a_n|\) can be made \_\_\_
edges.hint: Both terms hug \(L\), so their distance is at most \(2\varepsilon\).

edges.label: fix \(\varepsilon=1\) and \(N\): all terms past \(N\) lie in an interval of length \_\_\_
edges.hint: Centered at \(a_N\), radius \(1\) — total length is ___.

edges.label: so we can extract a \_\_\_ subsequence
edges.hint: BW guarantees this kind of subsequence from a bounded sequence.

edges.label: if subseq \(a_{n_k} \to L\) AND seq is Cauchy, the FULL seq has limit \_\_\_
edges.hint: Triangle inequality: \(|a_n - L| \le |a_n - a_{n_k}| + |a_{n_k} - L|\). Both small ⟹ \(a_n \to\) ___.

edges.label: in \(\mathbb{R}\): Cauchy \(\iff\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: The two notions coincide in \(\mathbb{R}\).
